Home/FHA 2–4 Unit Loans Florida
FHA 2–4 Unit Loans Florida

FHA loans for 2–4 unit properties in Florida

Buy a duplex, triplex, or fourplex with just 3.5% down by living in one unit — and let rental income help cover your mortgage. Here's how FHA multi-unit loans work.

2–4 Unit Properties

FHA loans for duplexes, triplexes & fourplexes in Florida

One of FHA's most powerful features: you can buy a 2-, 3-, or 4-unit property with as little as 3.5% down, as long as you live in one of the units. It's a popular "house-hacking" strategy for Florida buyers who want tenants to help cover the mortgage.

How it works

You occupy one unit as your primary residence and rent the others. FHA even lets you use a portion of the projected rental income to help you qualify, and loan limits are significantly higher for multi-unit properties — see the 2026 FHA loan limits for 2-, 3-, and 4-unit homes by county.

Duplex, triplex & fourplex

A duplex (2 units) is the most common entry point; triplex (3 units) and fourplex (4 units) increase both your borrowing limit and your rental income potential. Four units is the FHA maximum — five or more is considered commercial.

Things to know

You must intend to live in the property, it must pass an FHA appraisal, and multi-unit deals can have slightly different reserve and qualifying considerations. We'll walk you through the numbers for any 2–4 unit Florida property you're considering.

Questions & Answers

Frequently asked questions

Can I buy a duplex or fourplex with an FHA loan in Florida?+
Yes. FHA allows 2-, 3-, and 4-unit properties with as little as 3.5% down, provided you live in one of the units as your primary residence.
Can rental income help me qualify?+
Yes. FHA lets you use a portion of the projected rental income from the other units to help you qualify for a multi-unit purchase.
Are loan limits higher for multi-unit properties?+
Yes. FHA loan limits rise with the number of units — in most Florida counties the 2026 limit goes from $541,287 for one unit up to $1,041,125 for four units.
Do I have to live in the property?+
Yes. FHA multi-unit financing requires you to occupy one of the units as your primary residence; it's not for pure investment properties.
